summary: This lesson reviews irregular verbs in the imperfect (only ser/ir/ver are irregular) and introduces the pattern for irregular preterite verbs: the stem changes, endings mix -ar (for I and he/she) with -er/-ir (for the rest), and endings are unaccented. Tener (tuv-), estar (estuv-), and decir (dij-) are covered.

teachings.summary: The imperfect is very regular with only three irregular verbs: ser (era), ir (iba), and ver (veía). Ser uses 'era' (remember: an era is a line in the past). Ir uses iba (a mix of -aba and -ía). Ver keeps the extra -e from veo: veía.
teachings.rule: Ser: era, eras, éramos, eran. Ir: iba, ibas, íbamos, iban. Ver: veía, veías, veíamos, veían.
teachings.notes: The imperfect is the safest tense — almost entirely regular. You can fall back to it to avoid irregular preterite forms.

teachings.summary: Irregular preterite verbs share three traits: (1) the stem changes, (2) they use -e and -o endings (from -ar) for I and he/she, but -imos/-iste/-ieron (from -er/-ir) for the rest, and (3) the endings are NOT accented. Once you know the irregular stem, all endings follow the same pattern.
teachings.rule: Irregular preterite: changed stem + -e (I), -o (he/she), -imos (we), -iste (you informal), -ieron (they). No accent on endings.
teachings.notes: You can use the imperfect (tenía, estaba) to avoid irregular preterite forms when you need to get meaning across quickly.

teachings.summary: Review of regular preterite endings. -ar verbs: -é, -ó, -amos, -aron, -aste. -er/-ir verbs: -í, -ió, -imos, -ieron, -iste.
teachings.notes: The informal 'you' preterite has both -s and -t- in the ending (-aste, -iste).
